content.text: From Gap Analysis to Pathway Design

A skills gap analysis tells you what employees need to learn. A reskilling pathway tells you how they will learn it, in what sequence, at what pace, and with what support. The pathway design determines whether the investment in curriculum development converts to actual behaviour change — or whether employees complete modules and return to their old habits.

Effective reskilling pathways for Claude adoption share three characteristics: they are role-specific (not generic AI awareness), they centre on practice over content delivery, and they connect learning to the employee's actual work within the first week of starting.

attrs.alt: Reskilling pathway architecture — three tracks from Foundation through Practitioner to Builder

content.text: A three-track architecture serves the full spectrum of employee needs without over-investing in advanced content for users who only need foundational confidence:
Confident first use; knows what to try and what to avoid
Embedded daily user; can teach peers and design workflows
Completes Track 1 + manager nomination or self-selection
Software engineers, solution architects, data engineers
Can build Claude-powered tools, automations, and integrations via API
Technical role + Track 2 completion or equivalent experience

Do not put non-technical employees in Track 3. The API and agent content will not be applicable to their work and creates confusion about what Claude is for. Track 2 is the ceiling for the majority of knowledge workers, and it is a high ceiling.
Automated or manager-guided pathway assignment prevents the common failure mode of everyone defaulting to the lowest-effort track (Track 1) even when their role requires Track 2 depth:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def assign_reskilling_pathway(
    employee_name: str,
    role: str,
    skills_gap_severity: str,
    current_claude_usage: str,
    career_aspiration: str | None = None
) -> dict:
    """Assign an employee to the appropriate reskilling track and generate their learning plan."""
    prompt = f"""Assign this employee to a reskilling pathway for Claude adoption and create their plan.

Employee: {employee_name}
Role: {role}
Skills gap severity: {skills_gap_severity}
Current Claude usage: {current_claude_usage}
Career aspiration: {career_aspiration or "Not specified"}

Available tracks:
- Track 1 (Foundation): 4 hours, all employees, confident first use
- Track 2 (Practitioner): 12 hours, power users and champions, embedded + ready to teach
- Track 3 (Builder): 20 hours, engineers/architects, can build Claude-powered tools

Assign the employee and return JSON with:
- recommended_track: 1 | 2 | 3
- track_rationale: one sentence explaining why
- learning_schedule: {{weeks_to_complete: int, hours_per_week: float, recommended_format: str}}
- immediate_first_step: what they should do in the next 24 hours
- milestone_at_4_weeks: what success looks like at 4 weeks
- fast_track_eligible: true if they show indicators of moving to the next track within 3 months
- fast_track_trigger: what behaviour would trigger a fast-track upgrade"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

assignment = assign_reskilling_pathway(
    employee_name="Divya Nair",
    role="Senior Product Manager",
    skills_gap_severity="moderate",
    current_claude_usage="Uses ChatGPT occasionally for personal tasks; no Claude usage",
    career_aspiration="Interested in moving into an AI Product lead role"
)
print(f"Track: {assignment['recommended_track']} — {assignment['track_rationale']}")
print(f"Schedule: {assignment['learning_schedule']['hours_per_week']}h/week for {assignment['learning_schedule']['weeks_to_complete']} weeks")
print(f"First step: {assignment['immediate_first_step']}")
print(f"Fast track: {assignment['fast_track_eligible']} — {assignment['fast_track_trigger']}")

Each module in the pathway should follow the 30/40/30 rule: 30% concept (why this matters), 40% demonstration (Claude doing the task), 30% practice (the learner doing it themselves). Modules that are 90% presentation and 10% discussion do not build skill — they build familiarity at best.

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_module_content_outline(
    module_number: int,
    module_title: str,
    target_audience: str,
    duration_minutes: int,
    learning_objectives: list[str]
) -> dict:
    """Generate a detailed content outline for a reskilling module."""
    objectives_text = "\n".join(f"- {obj}" for obj in learning_objectives)
    prompt = f"""Create a detailed content outline for a Claude reskilling module.

Module {module_number}: {module_title}
Target audience: {target_audience}
Duration: {duration_minutes} minutes
Learning objectives (by the end, learners will be able to):
{objectives_text}

Design the module content outline including:
- opening_hook: how to start the session to generate immediate engagement (2-3 sentences)
- sections: list of {{title, duration_min, content_type, key_points: list[str], activity: str or null}}
  where content_type is "concept" | "demo" | "practice" | "discussion"
- hands_on_exercise: specific task learners complete during the module
- common_misconceptions: 2 misconceptions to proactively address
- closing_reflection: 1-2 reflection questions to end the session
- pre_work: optional pre-reading or task to complete before attending (max 15 min)
- facilitator_notes: 2-3 tips for the person delivering this module

Return as JSON."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=900,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

outline = generate_module_content_outline(
    module_number=4,
    module_title="Output Verification Habits",
    target_audience="All employees completing Foundation track",
    duration_minutes=45,
    learning_objectives=[
        "Identify three common Claude error patterns relevant to their role",
        "Apply the PACE verification checklist (Plausible, Accurate, Complete, Evidence-checkable) to any Claude output",
        "Know when to trust, verify, or discard a Claude output"
    ]
)
print(f"Hook: {outline['opening_hook']}")
print(f"\nSections ({len(outline['sections'])} total):")
for s in outline["sections"]:
    activity = f" + {s['activity']}" if s.get("activity") else ""
    print(f"  [{s['duration_min']} min, {s['content_type']}] {s['title']}{activity}")
print(f"\nCommon misconceptions:")
for m in outline["common_misconceptions"]:
    print(f"  • {m}")
What Claude Is and Is Not
Mental model: probabilistic language model vs. search vs. database
Spot the hallucination exercise (3 Claude outputs, find the error)
First Prompts — Your Role Starter Kit
Complete 3 role-specific prompts from the starter kit; add one of your own
Policy: what data can go in, enterprise gateway, zero-retention
Walk through 5 realistic scenarios; classify each as permitted / needs review / not permitted
PACE checklist: Plausible, Accurate, Complete, Evidence-checkable
Verify 2 Claude-generated outputs using the checklist; document one error found
Reskilling programme effectiveness is measured at four levels (mirroring Kirkpatrick):
— post-module survey (NPS + 2 open questions). Target: NPS ≥30 for each module.
— end-of-track assessment: 5-10 scenario questions with Claude context. Target: ≥80% pass rate.
— 30-day post-completion check: is the employee using Claude in their workflow? Rate: ≥70% of completers active after 30 days.
— aligned to the funnel: Track 1 completers should show higher Activated → Regular conversion than non-participants.

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_cohort_progress_report(
    cohort_name: str,
    cohort_track: int,
    completion_data: list[dict]
) -> str:
    """Generate a progress report for a reskilling cohort nearing completion."""
    completions = [d for d in completion_data if d.get("completed")]
    in_progress = [d for d in completion_data if not d.get("completed") and d.get("modules_done", 0) > 0]
    not_started = [d for d in completion_data if d.get("modules_done", 0) == 0]

    prompt = f"""Write a cohort progress report for a Claude reskilling programme.

Cohort: {cohort_name} (Track {cohort_track})
Total enrolled: {len(completion_data)}
Completed: {len(completions)} ({len(completions)/len(completion_data)*100:.0f}%)
In progress: {len(in_progress)}
Not started: {len(not_started)}

At-risk learners (in progress but falling behind schedule):
{json.dumps([d for d in in_progress if d.get("weeks_behind", 0) > 1], indent=2)}

Write a 3-paragraph progress report for the L&D team:
1. Overall health of the cohort (positive framing, cite completion rate)
2. Learners at risk — specific names and recommended actions for each
3. Recommendations for the final 2 weeks to maximise completion

Be specific and action-oriented. Under 200 words."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

report = generate_cohort_progress_report(
    cohort_name="Finance Team — Q2 2025",
    cohort_track=1,
    completion_data=[
        {"name": "Aisha Kamara", "modules_done": 4, "completed": True, "weeks_behind": 0},
        {"name": "Ben Osei", "modules_done": 4, "completed": True, "weeks_behind": 0},
        {"name": "Chioma Eze", "modules_done": 3, "completed": False, "weeks_behind": 0},
        {"name": "David Kim", "modules_done": 1, "completed": False, "weeks_behind": 2},
        {"name": "Elena Sousa", "modules_done": 0, "completed": False, "weeks_behind": 3},
        {"name": "Felix Wang", "modules_done": 4, "completed": True, "weeks_behind": 0},
        {"name": "Grace Oduya", "modules_done": 2, "completed": False, "weeks_behind": 1},
    ]
)
print(report)
Foundation Track Module 2 (first prompts) and Module 4 (verification)
Immediate Q&A; cohort bonding; facilitator can adapt to audience
Scheduling overhead; hard to scale; quality depends on facilitator
Foundation Track Modules 1 and 3 (concept-heavy, less practice)
On-demand; consistent quality; learner controls pace
Low completion rates without accountability structure
Deep practice with real work examples; peer learning
High time commitment; needs a skilled facilitator
All tracks — supplement to formal content
Role-specific examples; social proof; low cost
Quality varies by champion; not a substitute for structured content

Every track should culminate in a capstone project — a real deliverable that applies skills from across the track to an actual business problem. Capstones serve three purposes: they force integration of learning rather than isolated module completion; they produce a tangible artefact the learner's team can use; and they give L&D evidence of genuine skill transfer (not just module completion).

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_capstone_project_brief(
    track: int,
    learner_role: str,
    learner_department: str,
    completed_modules: list[str]
) -> dict:
    """Generate a personalised capstone project brief for a reskilling track completion."""
    modules_text = "\n".join(f"- {m}" for m in completed_modules)
    prompt = f"""Generate a personalised capstone project brief for a Claude reskilling Track {track} completion.

Learner: {learner_role} in {learner_department}
Modules completed:
{modules_text}

The capstone project must:
1. Be directly relevant to the learner's daily work in {learner_department}
2. Require them to use Claude skills from at least 3 of the completed modules
3. Produce a tangible deliverable their team can actually use
4. Be completable in 2-4 hours
5. Include a peer review component (learner shares deliverable with one colleague for feedback)

Return JSON with:
- project_title: string (specific, outcome-focused)
- context: 2-sentence setup describing the business scenario
- deliverable: exactly what the learner will produce
- steps: list of 4-6 specific steps with Claude instructions embedded
- modules_exercised: list of module titles this project draws on
- peer_review_prompt: what to ask the reviewing colleague to evaluate
- submission_criteria: how L&D will assess this for track completion"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=700,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

brief = generate_capstone_project_brief(
    track=2,
    learner_role="Procurement Analyst",
    learner_department="Supply Chain",
    completed_modules=[
        "What Claude Is and Is Not",
        "Advanced Prompting Techniques",
        "Supplier Analysis Use Cases",
        "Workflow Integration Lab",
        "Teaching Others — Champion Skills"
    ]
)
print(f"Project: {brief['project_title']}")
print(f"Context: {brief['context']}")
print(f"Deliverable: {brief['deliverable']}")
print("\nSteps:")
for i, step in enumerate(brief["steps"], 1):
    print(f"  {i}. {step}")
print(f"\nPeer review: {brief['peer_review_prompt']}")
Reskilling Programme Budget and ROI Model

Reskilling programmes require a budget case that accounts for both the direct costs (content development, facilitation, platform) and the indirect cost of employee time away from productive work. Claude can model the cost-ROI trade-off and calculate break-even timelines:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def build_reskilling_budget_model(
    programme_name: str,
    total_employees: int,
    track_distribution: dict,
    delivery_model: dict,
    fully_loaded_hourly_cost: float
) -> dict:
    """Model the cost and ROI of a reskilling programme."""
    # Calculate total learning hours
    track_hours = {"track_1": 4, "track_2": 12, "track_3": 20}
    total_learning_hours = sum(
        track_distribution.get(t, 0) * track_hours.get(t, 0)
        for t in track_hours
    )
    lost_productivity_cost = total_learning_hours * fully_loaded_hourly_cost

    prompt = f"""Model the cost and ROI for a Claude reskilling programme.

Programme: {programme_name}
Total employees to train: {total_employees}
Track distribution:
- Track 1 (Foundation, 4h): {track_distribution.get("track_1", 0)} employees
- Track 2 (Practitioner, 12h): {track_distribution.get("track_2", 0)} employees
- Track 3 (Builder, 20h): {track_distribution.get("track_3", 0)} employees

Delivery model and estimated costs:
{json.dumps(delivery_model, indent=2)}

Total employee learning hours: {total_learning_hours}
Lost productivity cost (at £{fully_loaded_hourly_cost}/hour): £{lost_productivity_cost:,.0f}

Build a cost-ROI model including:
- total_programme_cost: sum of content development + delivery + employee time
- cost_per_employee: float
- expected_productivity_gain_per_employee_per_week_hours: float (realistic, based on track)
- break_even_weeks: when cumulative productivity gains equal total cost
- 12_month_net_benefit: total gain minus total cost over 12 months
- key_assumptions: list of 3 assumptions in the model
- sensitivity: what would change the ROI most significantly

Return as JSON."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

model = build_reskilling_budget_model(
    programme_name="Claude Workforce Reskilling — Phase 1",
    total_employees=250,
    track_distribution={"track_1": 180, "track_2": 60, "track_3": 10},
    delivery_model={
        "content_development": "£45,000 (one-time — internal L&D + champion time)",
        "facilitation": "£12,000 (20 instructor-led sessions × £600 facilitation cost)",
        "platform_licensing": "£8,000/year (LMS + video hosting)",
        "champion_time": "£15,000 equivalent (150h of senior employee time @ £100/h)"
    },
    fully_loaded_hourly_cost=55.0
)
print(f"Total programme cost: {model['total_programme_cost']}")
print(f"Cost per employee: {model['cost_per_employee']}")
print(f"Break-even: {model['break_even_weeks']} weeks")
print(f"12-month net benefit: {model['12_month_net_benefit']}")
print("\nKey assumptions:")
for a in model["key_assumptions"]:
    print(f"  • {a}")

— a single half-day workshop does not produce behaviour change. The 30/40/30 module structure and cohort pacing exist precisely because spaced practice over 2–8 weeks drives retention and application. Any programme shorter than 4 hours spread over at least 2 weeks is awareness, not reskilling.

— generic 'intro to AI' modules that lack role-specific examples produce learners who understand Claude in the abstract but cannot apply it to their work on Monday morning. Every module must contain at least one example from the learner's function.
— module completion rates are an input metric. The output metric is whether learners are using Claude in their work 30 days after completion. Track completions but report on behaviour change.
No pathway back for lapsed learners

— employees who complete Track 1 and then don't use Claude for 60 days effectively need to restart. Build a 60-day re-engagement module (30 min refresh) to re-activate lapsed completers rather than putting them through the full track again.
Chain-of-thought, persona assignment, few-shot examples, iterative refinement
Mastery of 4 high-value use cases relevant to the learner's function
Embed Claude into an existing recurring process end-to-end
How to run a peer session; how to answer the 10 most common questions
Build a team workflow with Claude
Produce a use-case playbook and prompt template set for a real team workflow

Reskilling pathways convert skills gap analysis into structured learning journeys. A three-track architecture — Foundation (4h, all employees), Practitioner (12h, power users and champions), Builder (20h, engineers) — serves the full employee spectrum without over-investing in advanced content for users who need foundational confidence. Module design follows the 30/40/30 rule: concept, demonstration, practice — with hands-on exercises that connect to the employee's real work within the first session. Programme effectiveness is measured at all four Kirkpatrick levels, with behaviour change (Claude used after 30 days) as the primary outcome metric. Claude can assign pathway tracks, generate module outlines, and produce cohort progress reports — turning reskilling programme management from a manual reporting burden into an automated feedback loop.
